ligge.rule: ligge = to lie/be lying somewhere (no object): Bogen ligger på bordet. lægge = to lay/put (needs an object): Jeg lægger bogen på bordet. Past: lå vs lagde.

lægge.rule: lægge = to lay/put something (needs an object): Jeg lægger bogen på bordet. ligge = to lie/be lying (no object): Bogen ligger der. Past: lagde vs lå.

sidde.rule: sidde = to sit / be sitting (no object): Jeg sidder her. sætte = to set/put something (needs an object): Jeg sætter koppen ned.

sætte.rule: sætte = to set/put something (needs an object). sidde = to sit / be sitting (no object).

nogle.rule: Both sound the same ("NOH-en")! nogle = some / a few (positive statements): Jeg har nogle bøger. nogen = any / anyone (questions and negatives): Har du nogen bøger? Test: if "a few" fits, write nogle.

nogen.rule: Both sound the same ("NOH-en")! nogen = any / anyone (questions, negatives, singular): Er der nogen hjemme? nogle = some / a few (positive statements).

når.rule: Both mean "when". da = one single event in the past (Da jeg var barn...). når = repeated/habitual events or the future (Når jeg kommer hjem...). Rule of thumb: past one-time = da, everything else = når.

da.rule: da = when (one single past event): Da jeg så hende... når = when (repeated or future): Når det regner... da is also a flavor particle meaning "surely/come on": Det er da flot!

hvis.rule: hvis = if (condition): Hvis det regner, bliver jeg hjemme. om = if/whether (embedded question): Jeg ved ikke, om han kommer. If you could say "whether", use om.

om.rule: om = whether (Jeg ved ikke, om han kommer) — also the preposition "about/in". hvis = if (condition): Hvis det regner... If you could say "whether", use om.

sin.rule: sin/sit/sine = his/her OWN (points back to the subject): Han vasker sin bil = he washes his own car. Han vasker hans bil = he washes some other guy's car. English has no equivalent — this changes the meaning!

sit.rule: sin/sit/sine = his/her OWN (points back to the subject). sit for et-words: Hun elsker sit hus (her own house). hans/hendes = someone else's.

sine.rule: sine = his/her OWN (plural things): Han sælger sine bøger (his own books). But with a plural subject use deres: De sælger deres bøger.

hans.rule: hans = his (someone other than the subject): Han vasker hans bil = he washes ANOTHER man's car. For the subject's own things use sin/sit/sine: Han vasker sin bil.

hendes.rule: hendes = her (someone other than the subject): Hun tager hendes taske = she takes ANOTHER woman's bag. Her own bag: Hun tager sin taske.

og.rule: Both are pronounced "å" in speech, so even Danes mix them up in writing. og = and. at = to (before a verb: at spise) or "that" (han siger, at...).

at.rule: Both are pronounced "å" in speech. at = to (at spise) or "that". og = and. Writing og where at belongs is the classic Danish spelling mistake.

ville.rule: Both cover English "will". vil = want to / intend to. skal = it's arranged or required (Jeg skal arbejde i morgen = I'm working tomorrow, it's planned).

skulle.rule: skal = arranged/required future (Jeg skal til møde = I have a meeting). vil = want to / intend to. For fixed plans, Danes say skal, not vil.

gerne.rule: gerne = gladly / would like to: Jeg vil gerne have en kaffe (polite ordering). godt = well/fine — and in the set phrase jeg kan godt lide = I like.

godt.rule: godt = well/fine; in jeg kan godt lide = I like. gerne = gladly/willingly: jeg vil gerne = I would like. After vil use gerne, after kan use godt.

selv.rule: selv = myself/yourself/... (after the noun: mig selv) or "even" (before: selv børn). selve = "the very ..." before a definite noun: selve huset.

meget.rule: meget = much / very (uncountable things): meget mælk, meget godt. mange = many (countable things): mange bøger.

mange.rule: mange = many (countable): mange biler. meget = much/very (uncountable): meget vand.

mere.rule: mere = more of something uncountable: mere kaffe. flere = more of countable things: flere kopper.

flere.rule: flere = more (countable): flere venner. mere = more (uncountable): mere tid.

hun.rule: hun (she) and hund (dog) differ only by stød (the little glottal catch) — the d is silent! hund has stød, hun doesn't. Mixing them up is a beloved beginner story.

hund.rule: The d is silent: hund sounds like hun (she) plus stød (a glottal catch). Listen for the catch: hun'.

hen.rule: Short form = movement, long form = location. Hvor skal du hen? (where are you going?) vs Hvor har du været henne? (where have you been?).

henne.rule: henne = location (being somewhere), hen = movement (going somewhere).

hjem.rule: hjem = movement: Jeg går hjem (I'm going home). hjemme = location: Jeg er hjemme (I'm at home).

hjemme.rule: hjemme = location: Jeg er hjemme. hjem = movement: Jeg går hjem.

ud.rule: ud = movement out: Jeg går ud. ude = location outside: Jeg er ude.

ude.rule: ude = location outside: Han er ude. ud = movement out: Han går ud.

op.rule: op = movement up: Jeg går op. oppe = location up: Jeg er oppe (I'm up/awake).

oppe.rule: oppe = location up there: Han er oppe. op = movement up: Han går op.

ned.rule: ned = movement down: Jeg går ned. nede = location down there: Jeg er nede.

nede.rule: nede = location down there. ned = movement down.

ind.rule: ind = movement in: Kom ind! inde = location inside: Jeg er inde.

inde.rule: inde = location inside. ind = movement in: Kom ind!
